items.question: Which equation expresses the naive Bayes conditional independence assumption?
items.explanation: Naive Bayes factorizes the class-conditional joint into a product of per-feature terms given y.

items.question: For classification, naive Bayes predicts the class by:
options: argmax_y P(xᵢ|y) for the single strongest feature
items.explanation: The prior P(y) times the factored likelihood determines the posterior up to normalization; argmax selects the class.

items.question: The independence assumption in naive Bayes means:
options: Features are independent given the class y
items.answer: Features are independent given the class y
items.explanation: Conditional independence given y is the core assumption—not marginal independence of raw features.

items.question: Why use log probabilities in naive Bayes implementations?
options: Products of many small probabilities underflow; log sums are numerically stable
Bayes' theorem only works in log space
Log probabilities remove the need for a prior
items.answer: Products of many small probabilities underflow; log sums are numerically stable
items.explanation: argmax is unchanged by monotonic log, but log-domain avoids multiplying hundreds of probabilities toward zero.

items.question: An unseen word appears in a test document for Multinomial NB without smoothing. What happens?
options: P(x|y) becomes zero for that class, eliminating it from argmax
Only P(y) is used for that feature
items.answer: P(x|y) becomes zero for that class, eliminating it from argmax
items.explanation: Zero in any factor zeros the entire product; Laplace smoothing prevents this.
